coredata.dc:title: Cu2+-induced quenching and recovery of the luminescence of dopamine-conjugated carbon dots for sensing deferasirox in plasma
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chun-Chi
Huang, Po-Tsang
Shang Kou, Hwang-
Wu, Shou-Mei
coredata.dc:description: A novel dopamine-conjugated carbon dots (DA-CDs) sensor has been designed for estimation of deferasirox (DFX) in plasma of β-thalassemia. The carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by using citric acid and urea as precursors and heated in the microwave. Then, the CDs was further conjugated with dopamine to obtain the final product, DA-CDs. The fluorescence intensity was first quenched by copper ions and recovered when DFX was added. Through the calculation of fluorescence recovery, the concentration of DFX was therefore determined. The optimized conditions were found at Cu2+ concentration of 20 μM, reaction time of DFX with Cu2+ of 30 s, methanol percentage of 30 % and pH value of 11. The limit of detection was found at 600 ng/mL. The linearity response for DFX was within the concentration range from 1 μg/mL to 10 μg/mL with a correlation coefficient greater than 0.9975. In intra-day (n = 3) and inter-day (n = 5) assays, the RSD values were less than 8.7 %, and the absolute RE values were less than 4.8 %. The method was further applied to real plasma samples from 5 β-thalassemia patients, and the RSD in the detection of real samples for successive 3 times were below 4.71 %. When the data of real samples was compared with that by using capillary electrophoresis (CE)-UV, the RE were below 4.78 %. The simple sensing technique for detection of DFX in plasma has been optimized, validated and applied in real samples and can be served as a tool for clinical survey.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DA-CDs
Carbon dots
Deferasirox
Copper ions
Fluorescence quenching
Fluorescence recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The luminescent DA-CDs for sensing DFX
The specificity of the DA-CDs for sensing DFX

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of detection of DFX using dopamine-conjugated carbon dots.

Bar graph representing various optimised conditions in terms of fluorescent recovery (F-F0) values (A) Effect of different deproteination solvents, (B) Effect of the volume of deproteination solvent. [F = Fluorescence spectrum of DA-CDs in the presence of 20 μM Cu2+ and 10 μg/mL DFX at 440 nm, F0 = Fluorescence spectrum of DA-CDs in the presence of 20 μM Cu2+at 440 nm] [DA-CDs: 15 μg/mL, Cu2+concentration: 20 μM, reaction time of DFX with Cu2+: 30 s, methanol percentage: 30 %].

Bar graphs representing various optimised conditions of (A) Fluorescence quenching caused by different concentrations of copper ions, (B) Effect of reaction time of DFX with Cu2+, (C) Fluorescent recovery of different organic solvent added to the sample, (D) Fluorescent recovery of different methanol percentage. [F = Fluorescence of DA-CDs in the presence of 20 μM Cu2+ and 10 μg/mL DFX at 440 nm, F0 = Fluorescence of DA-CDs in the presence of 20 μM Cu2+at 440 nm, F1 = Fluorescence spectrum of DA-CDs at 440 nm] [DA-CDs: 15 μg/mL, Cu2+concentration: 20 μM, reaction time of DFX with Cu2+: 30 s, methanol percentage: 30 %].

(A) Bar graph representing the effect of pH value, (B) Optimized condition for the detection of DFX in plasma. [F = Fluorescence spectrum of DA-CDs in the presence of 20 μM Cu2+ and 10 μg/mL DFX at 440 nm, F0 = Fluorescence spectrum of DA-CDs in the presence of 20 μM Cu2+at 440 nm] [DA-CDs: 15 μg/mL, Cu2+concentration: 20 μM, reaction time of DFX with Cu2+: 30 s, methanol percentage: 30 %].

Bar graph representing the specificity of (A) 20 μM Cu2+ in comparison with 20 μM other metallic ions in the presence of 10 μg/mL DFX, (B) 10 μg/mL DFX in comparison with 10 μg/mL other chelating agents. [F = fluorescence spectrum of DA-CDs in the presence of 20 μM Cu2+ and 10 μg/mL chelating agents at 440 nm, F0 = fluorescence spectrum of DA-CDs in the presence of 20 μM metallic ions at 440 nm, F1 = fluorescence spectrum of DA-CDs at 440 nm].

(A) Fluorescence intensity recovery by adding different concentrations of DFX into plasma, and (B) five points selected for the calibration curve. [DA-CDs: 15 μg/mL, Cu2+concentration: 20 μM, reaction time of DFX with Cu2+: 30 s, deproteination solvent: 800 μL acetone, methanol percentage: 30 %, pH: 11].
Regression analysis for determination of deferasirox in plasma in an intraday (n = 3) and interday (n = 5) analysis (linear range: 1 μg/mL to 10 μg/mL).
Precision and accuracy for determination of deferasirox in plasma in an intraday (n = 3) and interday (n = 5) analysis (linear range: 1 μg/mL to 10 μg/mL).
Results of five plasma samples from five β-thalasemia patients.

head.ce:title: Cu2+-induced quenching and recovery of the luminescence of dopamine-conjugated carbon dots for sensing deferasirox in plasma

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University, 100, Shi-chuan 1st Rd., Kaohsiung, 807, Taiwan, ROC.
Corresponding author at: School of Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University, Taiwan, ROC.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel dopamine-conjugated carbon dots (DA-CDs) sensor has been designed for estimation of deferasirox (DFX) in plasma of β-thalassemia. The carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by using citric acid and urea as precursors and heated in the microwave. Then, the CDs was further conjugated with dopamine to obtain the final product, DA-CDs. The fluorescence intensity was first quenched by copper ions and recovered when DFX was added. Through the calculation of fluorescence recovery, the concentration of DFX was therefore determined. The optimized conditions were found at Cu2+ concentration of 20 μM, reaction time of DFX with Cu2+ of 30 s, methanol percentage of 30 % and pH value of 11. The limit of detection was found at 600 ng/mL. The linearity response for DFX was within the concentration range from 1 μg/mL to 10 μg/mL with a correlation coefficient greater than 0.9975. In intra-day (n = 3) and inter-day (n = 5) assays, the RSD values were less than 8.7 %, and the absolute RE values were less than 4.8 %. The method was further applied to real plasma samples from 5 β-thalassemia patients, and the RSD in the detection of real samples for successive 3 times were below 4.71 %. When the data of real samples was compared with that by using capillary electrophoresis (CE)-UV, the RE were below 4.78 %. The simple sensing technique for detection of DFX in plasma has been optimized, validated and applied in real samples and can be served as a tool for clinical survey.

ce:section.ce:para: β-Thalassemia is a highly prevalence hereditary disease induced from the absence of beta-globin chains. The patients are treated with two major therapeutic strategies, including bone marrow transplantation and long-term blood transfusion. The transplantation of bone marrow often accompanies with clinical rejection reaction, and thus blood transfusion is a kind of safe therapy for treating β-thalassemia. However, the iron ions will be accumulated in body due to the continuous blood transfusion and induce some syndromes, inducing hemochromatosis and peroxidative tissue damage, resulting from the iron overloading [1–3]. Therefore, high mortality will occur in the blood-transfusion β-thalassemia patients without well controlling the level of iron. To prevent iron overloading, iron-chelating agents are administrated for blood-transfusion patients to remove excess iron. Deferasirox (DFX), an oral iron chelator. DFX is a tridentate ligand which can bind with metallic ions. Three polar interactions of the molecule enable binding of DFX with iron in a 2:1 ratio. After the drug has bound to iron it is eliminated through urine or feces excretion [4–6]. DFX was generally well tolerated in clinical trials. The most common drug-related adverse events were nausea, rash and diarrhoea. In some cases, DFX has been associated with hepatic and renal toxicity. In order to understand its therapeutic efficacy and avoid toxicity, it becomes essential to monitor its plasma concentration.

Previously, some analytical techniques, such as CE-UV [7], HPLC [8], LC-MS [9], have been utilized for the detection of DFX in biological samples. However, they all have certain aspects that can be improved. In capillary electrophoresis, the LOD was 0.3 μg/mL, and the RSD and RE reached to 12 %. Regardless of accuracy and precision, the time consumption is too high. In the HPLC-UV methods, lower detection limit was obtained (LLOD = 1.07 μmol/mL), but the pretreatment process was much complicated and also extended the time required for analysis. In LC-MS, the lowest concentration can be determined (LLOQ = 0.04 μg/mL), but the cost of the instrument was very expensive which makes it unaffordable for some academic institutions. Therefore, to develop a novel method for the detection of DFX with simple pretreatment and fast analysis becomes a necessity for clinical treatment for patients with β-thalasemia.

Recently, sensor has become a trend in the analytical strategies, due to its fast analysis, simple operation and small consumption of the reagents. A lots of nanomaterial are utilized as fluorescent materials or quenchers for sensing analytes. Among those, carbon dots (CDs) is one class of nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm. Fascinating properties of CDs include good biocompatibility, water solubility, cell permeability and strong luminescence [10–12]. With so many advantages, the CD-based sensors can be used for visual monitoring of DNA, glutathione, various metallic ions and other materials [13–18]. They have also been widely used in other fields like bioimaging or biomedicine delivery system [19,20]. Sometimes, conjugation of certain functional material on the surface of CDs would be completed to raise the specific binding between CDs and analytes. In this study we have designed a new sensor where we have used dopamine-conjugated carbon dots for the detection of DFX in human plasma. The conjugation of dopamine with carbon dots have been studied previously, but however have not been used for the detection of drug in biological fluid, DFX [21,22]. Additionally, the CDs utilized in each research was different. Here we have used it for the first time to detect DFX by its affinity to copper ions with the o-semiquinone structure from its free base form. The fluorescence intensity of dopamine-conjugation CDs was quenched by the binding with copper ions and recovered when DFX was added. The concentration of DFX could be quickly determined through the calculation of fluorescence recovery. This simple and fast sensing technique for detection of DFX in plasma could be served as a tool for clinical survey of DFX level in β-Thalassemia patients.

All chemicals used were of pure analytical grade. Citric acid, urea, sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), acetone, acetonitrile (ACN), methanol (MeOH), 1-butanol and ethyl acetate were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). DFX was purchased from AK scientific (California, USA). Dopamine hydrochloride, silver nitrate (AgNO3), lithium sulfate (Li2SO4), deferoxamine (DFO) and deferiprone (DFR) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St, Louis, MO, USA). N-Hydroxysuccinimide, 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodimide), meso-2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA), nitrilotriacetic acid (NTA), d-Penicillamine (DPA) and 2,3-dimercapto-1-propanol (BAL) were bought from Tokyo Chemical Industry (Tokyo, Japan). Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), calcium chloride (CaCl2) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) were from Mallinckrodt Baker (Phillipsburg, USA). Ferric chloride anhydrous (FeCl3) and ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2∙4H2O) were from SHOWA Chemical Industry (Japan). The water used in this experiment, Milli-Q water, was obtained from the Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA (resistivity of 18.2 M Ω. Cm at 25 °C).

The microwave used to synthesize the DA-CDs was a JEOL JEM-2100 F model obtained from JEOL, Peabody, MA, USA. The fluorescence spectrophotometer used in this instrument was a Hitachi F-4500 model obtained from Tokyo, Japan. The Freeze centrifuge drier used to dry the organic solvent during extraction was centrifugal vaporizer CVE 200D model obtained from Tokyo Rikakikai corporation. LTD, Japan. The FT-IR spectrophotometer was a Nicolet 6700 model obtained from Thermo, MA, USA. Other instruments included ultrasonic oscillator (Branson 5510, Branson, Danbury, CT, USA), cooling centrifuge (Centrifuge 3500 Kubota, Osaka, Japan) and pH meter (TOA HM-30 V).

The CDs were synthesized by reacting citric acid (CA) and urea in microwave irradiation. At first, 6.006 g of urea and 21.014 g of CA was weighed into 100 mL volumetric flask. Subsequently, 90 mL of Milli-Q water was added and sonicated for 30 min with occasional shaking. Once all the CA and urea have been dissolved completely, the volume was made up to 100 mL using Milli-Q water and kept for sonication for 30 min. The both concentration of CA and urea were 1 M, respectively. This solution was distributed to 8 microwave tubes and heated at 200℃ for 30 min (ramp time: 30 min). The product was passed through a filter of 0.22 μm before dialysis. Dialysis was performed on the membrane of 3500 Da against water for 24 h. After dialysis, the CDs products were further reacted with N-Hydroxysuccinimide and 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodimide). In this reaction, 80 mg 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodimide, 80 mg N-Hydroxysuccinimide, 8 mL 10X PBS buffer and 72 mL CD solution was added into a beaker and stirred for 2 h at room temperature. Afterwards, 80 mg of dopamine hydrochloride was added and stirred for 24 h at room temperature to obtain the final product, dopamine-conjugated carbon dots (DA-CDs). The DA-CDs was further dialysed for 24 h by using the same membrane against water.

The DA-CDs were characterized using the dynamic laser scattering (DLS) to check the particle size and zeta potential of the DA-CDs. Following this the transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were also utilized to find the topography of the DA-CDs. Besides, the FT-IR spectrum was also used to see the functional groups of the DA-CDs products.

The buffer solution used for analysis was phosphate buffer. At first 0.89 g of Na2HPO4 was weighed in a 50 mL volumetric flask. 40 mL of autoclaved deionized water was added and kept for sonication for 15 min. After the buffer salt has been dissolved completely, the volume was made up to 50 mL using the same autoclaved deionized water and left for sonication for more 15 min. Once the buffer solution is completely prepared, the pH of the buffer was adjusted to 11 using 1 M NaOH. The phosphate buffer was prepared at 100 mM and later diluted to 10 mM for preparation of samples.

Stock solution of DFX was prepared in MeOH to make the concentration of 1 mg/mL. The working solution of DFX (100 μg/mL) was prepared by further dilution using MeOH. 20 μL of DFX (100 μg/mL) was withdrawn and added to the plasma (180 μL) in an eppendorf to make the final concentration of DFX10 μg/mL. The plasma was collected from a healthy volunteer not taking DFX. 800 μL of acetone was added to this mixture (200 μL plasma sample) and then vortexed. After deproteination, the eppendorf were centrifuged for 15 min at 13,000 rpm. The supernatant is collected for analysis. In this work, the plasma was only simple pretreated with organic solvent for the sensing of DFX. Additionally, 200 μL plasma samples from β-thalassemia patients under DFX treatment were taken, and pretreated according the above procedure.

The original concentration of the DA-CDs was 660 μg/mL, and then diluted to about 15 μg/mL for the sensing of DFX. The total of 200 μL reaction solution for fluorescent detection contained 20 μM Cu2+ ions, 50 μL of the supernatant form plasma, 15 μg/mL DA-CDs, 30 % methanol in 10 mM phosphate solution (pH 11). The conditions for fluorescence spectrophotometer were set about the following: excitation wavelength was at 335 nm and the emission range was between 400–600 nm. Both excitation and emission slit were set at 10.0 nm. The PMT was set at 700 V.

In this study, the investigation started by checking the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength of DA-CD. After that, copper ions were added to DA-CDs to check its quenching effect. Additionally, in order to investigate the quenching effects of other metal ions, various metal ions were also added into the solution to detect the fluorescence, including K+, Na+, Li+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+. The fluorescence intensity of DA-CDs could be quenched by copper ions and then recovered when DFX was added. The concentration of DFX was determined through the calculation of fluorescence recovery. In order to try the selectivity of this sensing technique, different chelating agents, such as deferoxamine (DFO), deferiprone (DFR), meso-2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA), nitrilotriacetic acid (NTA), d-Penicillamine (DPA), 2,3-dimercapto-1-propanol (BAL) and ethylenediamine tetra-acetic acid (EDTA) were also surveyed in this system. The selectivity test of the various metal ions and chelating agents in the DA-CD was accomplished in the standard and plasma samples. The plasma samples spiked with various metal ions and chelating agents were pretreated according to Section 2.5. Although detection of DFX was possible, however, parameters were needed to be optimized to find the best condition. Thus, the parameters, including concentration of copper ions, reaction time of DFX with copper ions, deproteination solvent and its volume, the organic solvent added to the solution and its percentage, and the pH value of buffer were optimised.

The calibration curve of DFX in plasma was established within the concentration range from 1 μg/mL to 10 μg/mL. The calibration graph was established with the fluorescence recovery caused by DFX at 440 nm as ordinate (Y) and concentration of DFX in μg/mL as abscissa (X). Different concentrations were chosen for analysis of intra- and inter-day precision and accuracy. Intra-day was defined as three repeated analyses in a single day (n = 3) and inter-day consisted of repeated analyses over five different days (n = 5). Relative recoveries of analytes from the plasma samples were determined to check the matrix effect. The limits of detection (LOD) were determined by considering the fluorescence signal of the concentration of the analytes where mean of the fluorescence spectra is three times higher than the standard deviation of the blank. The selectivity of the copper ions and DFX was checked using various metallic ions and chelating agents.

The synthesis of CDs was carried out using CA and urea dissolved in Milli-Q water and then heated in the microwave for 30 min (200℃) to yield the initial product, CDs. Subsequently, the CDs went through a series of reaction to obtain the final product, DA-CDs as described above. The DA-CD was characterised by checking their particle size, charge, topography and functional groups. The DLS was used to check the zeta potential of the DA-CDs. The zeta potential of DA-CDs was -25.47 mv (Fig. S1A) and that of CDs was -7.75 mV (Fig. S1B) in the solution of PBS buffer (10 mM Na2HPO4, pH 11). The surface charge of DA-CDs was more negative than that of CDs which is already negative. The data supported the dopamine was successfully conjugated on the surface of CDs. The TEM data showed that the shapes of DA-CDs were spherical without aggregation (Fig. S2A), and the DLS spectrum showed that the average size of the DA-CDs was 5.14 nm which was under acceptable range (Fig. S2B). The FT-IR was utilized for detection of the functional groups in product as well as the precursors. In comparison of CDs and its precursors, citric and urea (Fig. S3A), it was found that the three materials have similar peaks at around 3200–3400 (NH, (OH), 1500–1700 ((CO), which proved that CDs contained the functional groups of citric acid and urea. Besides, the same peak showed lower intensity in the spectrum of DA-CDs, which was supported that the functional groups from the precursors were shielded by the conjugation of dopamine (Fig. S3A and S3B). The excitation wavelength of DA-CDs was screened from 305 nm to 365 nm and the emission was set at 400 nm–600 nm. It was observed that at 335 nm the DA-CDs showed the best excitation and its best emission spectrum at 440 nm (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The luminescent DA-CDs for sensing DFX
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the luminescent DA-CDs was designed for sensing DFX in the plasma of β-thalassemia patients. The mechanism was as shown in Fig. 1 . When the DA-CDs was excited at 335 nm, they possessed a strong fluorescence at 440 nm. In the basic environment (pH 11), the hydroxyl groups of the catechol structure become deprotonated, and turns into the o-semiquinone, enabling binding of metal ions. Therefore, various metal ions including Cu2+, K+, Na+, Li+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ were mixed with DA-CDs, and the data was as described in Section 3.4. However, the Cu2+ could induce the maximum fluorescent quenching of DA-CDs, although dopamine could specifically complex with iron ions. Thus, when copper ions are added, copper ions bind to the o-semiquinone of the DA-CDs, causing electron transfer from the negatively charged DA-CDs to the positively charged copper ions, resulting in decrease of ﬂuorescence intensity. As soon as addition of DFX, DFX forms a complex with copper ions in a 2 to 1 ratio, and hence prevents the binding of dopamine with copper ions and results in recovery of fluorescence intensity. Therefore, through the calculation of fluorescence recovery, the concentration of DFX in plasma could be determined. Because a lot of parameters should be investigated to obtain the optimal fluorescence recovery for sensing DFX in plasma, the following was the optimization of this sensor technique.

In this research, the real plasma which was difficultly applicable in sensor due to its complicated contents was utilized as the sample matrix. In order to simplify the pretreatmant of the plasma sample, simple deproteination of the plasma by using certain organic solvent was utilized to obtain the matrix for analysis. Different organic solvents including acetone, acetonitrile, methanol, ethyl acetate and 1-butanol were added to 200 μL plasma sample to investigate. The data showed acetone was the optimal solvent used for deproteination. (Fig. 2 A). Different volumes of acetone were also investigated to find the optimized condition. The volume from 200 μL to 1000 μL were added to 200 μL plasma sample to evaluate. (Fig. 2B). Because the volume of the organic solvent was not enough, the interference in plasma could not be removed clearly resulting in the poor fluorescence quenching. That was supported the interference would interact with copper ions and decrease the binding of copper ions and DA-CDs. However, when the volume of deproteination solvent increased, there was a decrease of fluorescence recovery due to the dilution of DFX in plasma. Hence, 800 μL acetone was chosen as the deproteination solvent.

The total of 200 μL reaction solution was taken for fluorescent detection. In order to obtain the optimized fluorescent quenching and recovery, several parameters were investigated. The first parameter to be optimized was the copper ions concentrations used for fluorescence quenching. The quenching effect increased upon the increase of copper ions concentrations, which provided the space for fluorescence recovery when DFX was added. When higher concentration of copper ions were added, more copper ions bound to the o-semiquinone structure of dopamine, causing electron transfer from DA-CDs to copper ions resulting in fluorescence quenching. However, when the concentration of copper ion was too high, the dopamine on the edge of CDs was saturated with copper ions and the quenching effect reached its maximum. Therefore, 5–25 μM of copper ions was mixed with the DA-CDs to obtain the maximum quenching effect. The data was as shown in Fig. 3 A. As increasing the concentration of copper ions, the fluorescence of the DA-CDs could be more quenched. When 20 μM copper ions was added, the quenching effect reached to maximum, and that was chosen as the optimized copper ions concentration. In order to obtain the best fluorescence recovery when the DFX was added to capture the copper ions from the quenching DA-CDs, the reaction time of DFX with Cu2+ was measured. The results showed that the reaction between DFX and Cu2+ was done very quickly within 30 s and the fluorescence intensity remained stable for 4 min. (Fig. 3B).

Additionally, it was found that addition of organic solvents to the matrix had a big influence on fluorescence intensity of the DA-CDs. That was supported the organic solvent in the matrix would affect the electron transfer of DA-CDs. Therefore, different organic solvents were investigated to find the optimized fluorescence of DA-CDs. The best results were yielded when methanol was added to the matrix (Fig. 3C). That was supported the methanol has more hydrogen bonding on the surface of DA-CDs than others, resulting in the high fluorescence of DA-CDs in the presence of methanol [23,24]. The percentage of methanol added to the matrix was also studied from 0% to 40 % and the best results were yielded at 30 %. However, when the methanol was increased to a certain percentage, the increase of fluorescence intensity declined (Fig. 3D). Finally, 30 % methanol was added into the matrix.

In this experiment, pH value of buffer also played a significant role in the detection of DFX. Therefore, the pH values of PBS buffer (10 mM Na2HPO4) from 9 to 12 were investigated (Fig. 4 A). At pH 11, the efficiency of fluorescence quenching and recovery reaches the highest point. That was due to the basic environment deprotonated the hydroxyl groups of dopamine’s catechol structure, enabling binding with copper ions which caused fluorescence quenching and thus providing space for recovery. However, when the pH value was too high, the fluorescent recovery became poor. The so high pH value might affect the binding of DFX with copper ions, resulting the decrease of fluorescent recovery. Finally, the optimized conditions in the total of 200 μL reaction solution were found at Cu2+ concentration: 20 μM, reaction time of DFX with Cu2+: 30 s, methanol percentage: 30 %, pH value of buffer: 11. The Fig. 4B showed the sensing of DFX under optimized conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The specificity of the DA-CDs for sensing DFX
ce:section.ce:para: In the development of sensing techniques, the specificity is a very important issue. Hence, various metal, including Cu2+, K+, Na+, Li+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions were also mixed with DA-CDs to evaluate the effect of fluorescent quenching and recovery in the presence of DFX. The data was as shown in Fig. 5 A. Additionally, these various metal ions were also spiked into the plasma sample to investigate the quenching and recovery effect (as shown in Fig. S5A). The data showed the Cu2+ could make the maximum fluorescent quenching and recovery of the DA-CDs in comparison of various metal ions, and the effects in standards were similar with those in plasma samples. In previous researches, they reported the o-semiquinone of dopamine would capture the ferric ion [25–27]. Indeed, the ferric and ferrous ions would also be bound with the DA-CDs, resulting in the fluorescent quenching. However, the quenching efficiency of ferric or ferrous ions was lower than copper ions resulting in the recovery efficiency of ferric or ferrous ions was lower than copper ions. This might be because the fluorescence of CDs could be specifically quenched by copper ions. In previous researches [28–32], the CDs could be utilized for specific detection of copper ions that indicated the certain CDs could have a high affinity or interaction with copper ions, and in this study, the conjugation of dopamine on the surface of the CDs could increase the binding with copper ions. Thus, Cu2+ was selected for fluorescent quenching of DA-CDs.

After choosing the Cu2+ as the quenching agent, the various chelating agents, including deferoxamine (DFO), deferiprone (DFR), meso-2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA), nitrilotriacetic acid (NTA), d-Penicillamine (DPA), 2,3-dimercapto-1-propanol (BAL) and ethylenediamine tetra-acetic acid (EDTA) were also evaluated to test the specificity of the DA-CDs for sensing DFX. This selectivity test of various chelating agents was carried on in the standard solutions (Fig. 5B) and plasma samples (Fig. S5B). From the data, the DFX had the highest fluorescent recovery from the Cu2+ induced quenching DA-CDs. Although DFX is an oral iron chelator for blood-transfusion patients to remove excess iron, it also has the binding affinity for Cu2+ [33,34]. Therefore, the DFX could chelate the Cu2+ to make the fluorescence of DA-CDs recover. However, other chelating agents also have the ability for complexing the Cu2+, but the DFX show the highest potential to recover the fluorescence of quenching DA-CDs. That was supported, first, the sensing system was accomplished at pH 11, and previous research reported the DFX had higher binding affinity with Cu2+ than DFO and DFR under such pH value [33]. Second, the complexing structure of DFX with Cu2+ (2 DFX to 1 metal ion) was tridentate structure that might cover the Cu2+ well in the core of the complexing structure to avoid that Cu2+ induced the fluorescent quenching of DA-CDs [35]. That is meaning the quenching DA-CDs by using Cu2+ could be utilized for specifically detecting the DFX in plasma.

The developed method was validated under optimised conditions described above. Under optimised conditions it was found that the LOD of DFX was 600 ng/mL in plasma (X-XB = 3SDB; X, the fluorescence of quenching DA-CDs by adding DFX; XB, the fluorescence of quenching DA-CDs without DFX; SDB, the SD value of the fluorescence of quenching DA-CDs without DFX). The fluorescence recovery of quenching DA-CDs by adding different concentrations (0.6−20 μg/mL) of DFX into plasma was recorded in Fig. 6 A. When the concentration of DFX spiked to plasma was too high, the fluorescence recovery became low. Steinhauser et al. reported that stable dinuclear Cu complexes were present at lower DFX concentration because two DFX ligands would coordinate two Cu(II) atoms [36]. The dimeric complex is centrosymmetric and stabilized by some stacking interactions between the triazole and the pyridine moieties of DFX. However, at higher DFX concentration, the structure might change to unstable mononuclear Cu complexes (where two DFX ligands coordinate one Cu(II)) resulting in the poor fluorescence recovery of the DA-CDs. Nevertheless, the fluorescence recovery of quenching DA-CDs was linear versus the concentration of DFX from 1 to 10 μg/mL added in plasma (Fig. 6B). The regression equations of intra-day and inter-day analysis were calculated in triplicate on a single day (n = 3) and on five consecutive days (n = 5). As shown in Table. 1 , results of the linear regression equations indicated that high linearity calibration plots were linear (r > 0.9975) over a range of 1–10 μg/mL for DFX in plasma. The relative standard deviation (RSD) and relative error (RE) of the sensing technique were used to evaluate precision and accuracy, based on statistical determinations (n = 3) of each analyte at three different concentrations (as shown in Table 2 ). In intra-day and inter-day assays, the RSD values were less than 8.7 %, and the absolute RE values were less than 4.8 % showing the good precision and accuracy of this sensing technique for detection of DFX on plasma.

After the method has been validated, it has been applied to monitor the plasma levels of DFX from 5 patients with β-thalassemia. In this study, only the free DFX concentration in plasma is being measured, because the iron-complexed fraction will not be able to recover the fluorescence of DA-CDs. Their concentrations have been determined by previous capillary electrophoresis strategy [7], and the data found by this sensing technique was compared with those. The data was as shown in Table 3 . In the detection of the 5 real plasma samples by this sensing technique, the RSD values for successive 3 times were below 4.71 % that was closed to that in method validation. Additionally, when the DFX concentrations found by this sensing technique was compared with those by using CE-UV, the RE values were less than 4.78 %. That is meaning this sensing technique could provide the accurate DFX concentrations in real plasma samples and can be served as a tool for clinical survey of DFX.

A novel and simple sensor based on the DA-CDs has been developed for the estimation of DFX in plasma samples. The plasma which was difficultly applicable in sensor due to its complicated contents was utilized and simple pretreated with acetone for deproteination before detection of DFX. The fluorescence intensity of DA-CDs was first quenched by copper ions and then recovered when DFX was added. Through the calculation of fluorescence recovery, the concentration of deferasirox was determined. The fluorescence quenching DA-CDs by using copper ions was utilized for specific detection of DFX. The method has been optimized and validated, and presented the good linearity precision and accuracy. The sensing technique was further applied to monitor the plasma levels of DFX from 5 patients with β-thalassemia, and the DFX concentrations obtained from this sensing technique showed good corresponding to those detected from the CE-UV technique which has been applied to monitor the DFX concentrations in clinical. This simple and novel sensing technique was feasible for serving as a tool to easily monitor the DFX concentration in plasma for clinical survey.

Chun-Chi Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. Po-Tsang Huang: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Hwang- Shang Kou: Supervision, Project administration. Shou-Mei Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We extend our deep and sincere thanks to volunteers who kindly contributed samples that were crucial to this study. We gratefully acknowledge the support of the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 105-2113-M-037-012-MY3 and MOST 106-2113-M-037-020-MY2) for the funding of this work and the help of Kaohsiung Medical University Chung-Ho Memorial Hospital.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.127916.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Cnaan, Survival and complications in patients with thalassemia major treated with transfusion and deferoxamine, Haematologica 89 (2004) 1187-1193.

Ther. 37 (2015) 1751-1760.

Méndez Román, M. Vega-Cartagena, S.A. Loza-Rosas, X.R. Acevedo, A.D. Tinoco, Iron and Copper Intracellular Chelation as an Anticancer Drug Strategy, Inorganics 6 (2018) 126.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Complex formation of ICL670 and related ligands with FeIII and FeII

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Steinhauser, U. Heinz, M. Bartholomä, T. Weyhermüller, H. Nick, K. Hegetschweiler, Complex Formation of ICL670 and Related Ligands with FeIII and FeII, Eur. J. Inorg. Chem. 21 (2004) 4177-4192.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chun-Chi Wang is currently an assistant professor in the School of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University. He obtained his Ph.D. from the College of Pharmacy at Kaohsiung Medical University in 2009 with Dr. Shou-Mei Wu. His research interests are focused on capillary electrophoresis, pharmaceutical analysis, gene analysis, and design of sensing probes, synthesis of nanomaterials and development of biosensors.

Po-Tsang Huang obtained his master degree from the College of Pharmacy at Kaohsiung Medical University in the July of 2019 with Dr. Chun-Chi Wang. His research interests are focused on synthesis of nanomaterials and development of biosensors.

Hwang- Shang Kou is currently an assistant professor in the School of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University. He obtained his Ph.D. degree from the department of Pharmacy at Kaohsiung Medical College in 1996. His research interests are pharmaceutical and toxicant analysis, and chromatographic analysis (GC & HPLC).

Shou-Mei Wu is currently a professor in College of Pharmacy at Kaohsiung Medical University, and seconded as the dean of the Taiwan Food and Drug Administration, Ministry of Health and Welfare. She obtained her Ph.D. from the College of Pharmacy at Kaohsiung Medical University in 1994. Her research interests are focused on nanotechniques, pharmaceutics, instrumental analysis, bioanalytical technology (drugs, genes, and proteins), toxicology, and cosmetics.
